risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were randomly assigned to the dietary support intervention (the modified Mediterranean diet) or the social support control condition." Comment: The report states that participants were randomly assigned, but it does not provide any detail on the method used to generate the allocation sequence (e.g., computer-generated random numbers, random blocks, stratification, or other random procedure). Under the stated criteria, 'randomly assigned' alone without context clues is insufficient to judge sequence generation as truly random, so this remains unclear.

Summary: The report states that participants were "randomly assigned" to the dietary support intervention or social support control, but it does not describe any method used to conceal the allocation sequence from the person enrolling participants. There is no explicit mention of central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, sequentially numbered identical containers, or any other mechanism preventing foreknowledge of the next assignment. Comment: Random assignment alone is insufficient to judge allocation concealment, so this remains unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Moreover, while the outcome assessors were blinded to treatment allocation, trial participants were not. It is unclear whether expectation bias may have influenced the measured outcomes of this analysis in some way." Comment: Participants were explicitly not blinded, and the intervention involved face-to-face dietary counselling versus befriending, so personnel and participants would likely know allocation. Because the main outcome here includes self-reported sickness absence and depression-related measures that could plausibly be influenced by expectations and behaviour, the study is at high risk of performance bias.

Quote: "While the outcome assessors were blinded to treatment allocation, trial participants were not." Comment: The paper explicitly states that outcome assessors were blinded. The outcomes relevant to this economic evaluation were mainly AQoL-8D/QALYs and self-reported resource use/productivity data. Although some components were self-reported by participants (who were not blinded), the study also reports blinded outcome assessors, and the main utility/QALY measurement is a standardized questionnaire rather than clinician judgement. On balance, for outcome assessment blinding this is judged low risk for the assessor-based outcomes reported here.

Summary: The paper reports that the reference case used intention-to-treat and multiple imputation for missing data, and the amount of missing endpoint data was limited to 33% for health care resource use questions at 12 weeks. It also states that complete-case analyses gave similar results to the imputed ITT analysis. Comment: Although some follow-up cost data were missing, the use of ITT with plausible multiple imputation and concordant complete-case sensitivity analyses means attrition/missing data are unlikely to have biased the result materially.
